title: Separating Content and Style
concept: A CNN naturally separates content and style: deep layers capture object layout (content), while the correlations between filter activations at shallow layers capture texture patterns (style, via the Gram matrix).

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the key difference between content and style in an image?

options.text: Content is layout/objects; style is color/texture/brushstrokes
Content is file size; style is resolution
There is no difference; they are the same thing

exercises.explain: Yes! Content is the 'what', style is the 'how'.

cards.title: Two different questions
cards.body: If you ask a CNN 'what objects are in this image?' you get content features (deep layers). If you ask 'what textures and color patterns repeat here?' you get style features (shallow layer correlations).

cards.title: Content & Style Representations
cards.body: Content representation comes from deep CNN layer activations (they preserve spatial layout). Style representation comes from the Gram matrix — correlations between different filter activations at a layer, capturing texture regardless of position.

cards.title: Melody vs. instrument mix
cards.body: Content is like a song's melody (the specific notes and structure). Style is like the instrument mix (which instruments play together, their timbre). The Gram matrix captures 'which filters fire together', like which instruments blend, ignoring exactly where in the song.

cards.title: Building a Gram matrix
cards.body: At a layer with 64 filters, compute how each filter's activation map correlates with every other filter's activation map across all spatial positions. This produces a 64x64 matrix — the style signature of that layer, independent of object position.

cards.title: Style ignores exact position
cards.body: It's a common misconception that style captures where things are. The Gram matrix deliberately averages out spatial position, keeping only which patterns co-occur — that's why the swirl pattern from Starry Night can appear anywhere in your bridge photo, not just where it appeared in the original painting.

cards.title: Content vs. Style Extraction
cards.body: How the CNN captures each ingredient:

left.text:
Deep layer raw activations
Preserves spatial layout
Answers 'what is where'

right.text:
Gram matrix of filter correlations
Position-independent
Answers 'what textures co-occur'

exercises.prompt: What mathematical tool captures 'style' in Neural Style Transfer?

options.text: The Gram matrix, which measures correlations between filter activations
The raw pixel values of the image
The image's file size in bytes

exercises.explain: Correct! The Gram matrix captures texture correlations.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The Gram matrix preserves exact spatial position of features.
exercises.explain: False. The Gram matrix deliberately discards spatial position, keeping only which filters co-occur.

exercises.sentence: Content representation comes from deep layer activations, while style comes from the ___ matrix.
exercises.explain: The Gram matrix captures style by measuring filter correlations.

exercises.prompt: Why can a swirl pattern from a style image appear anywhere in the output, not just its original position?

options.text: Because the Gram matrix discards spatial position, capturing only co-occurring textures
Because the network randomly shuffles pixels
Because JPEG compression moves patterns around

exercises.explain: Correct! Position-independence is exactly why textures can transfer freely.

exercises.prompt: If you wanted a style transfer result that captures Van Gogh's swirling brush texture but ignores exactly where his brushstrokes were positioned in the original painting, which representation should you use?

options.text: The Gram matrix of style-layer activations, since it captures texture correlations independent of position
The raw deep-layer content activations, since they preserve exact object position
The original painting's pixel coordinates directly
The bounding boxes of brushstroke regions

exercises.explain: Exactly! The Gram matrix is built for exactly this. Next: how the actual optimization loop combines both losses.
